title: Sequential UV mutagenesis and CO<sub>2</sub> acclimation reprogram metabolic carbon allocation in Euglena gracilis.
author_string: Lv, Qi, Du, Xinxin, Wang, Huan, Fan, Yawen, Song, Chunhua et al.
abstract: Euglena gracilis is promising for CO<sub>2</sub> bioutilization, but its metabolic response to elevated CO<sub>2</sub> is poorly characterized. Here, the LE-ZW mutant, obtained via UV mutagenesis and CO<sub>2</sub> acclimation, was evaluated under 5% and 10% CO<sub>2</sub>. LE-ZW exhibited 7% higher cell density and a 61% increase in photosynthetic carbon fixation at 10% CO<sub>2</sub> compared with 5% CO<sub>2</sub>. Carbon allocation shifted with CO<sub>2</sub> concentration: lipid accumulation peaked at 20.57% under 5% CO<sub>2</sub>, while paramylon content reached 37.37% under 10% CO<sub>2</sub>. Protein levels increased by 4% under high CO<sub>2</sub> (vs 5% CO<sub>2</sub> in LE-ZW). Transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses revealed extensive metabolic reprogramming: vs the wild type at 5% CO<sub>2</sub>, LE-ZW had 923 upregulated genes and 243 increased metabolites (enriched in TCA cycle and fatty acid biosynthesis, including 1.38-fold higher eicosapentaenoic acid); vs 5% CO<sub>2</sub> at 10%, it had 396 upregulated genes and 20 increased metabolites (enriched in Calvin cycle, GS-GOGAT pathway, and aminoacyl-tRNA biosynthesis). These changes identified regulatory hubs (glycolysis-gluconeogenesis nodes, acetyl-CoA supply, glucose-6-phosphate partitioning) that redirected carbon flux between lipids and paramylon. Antioxidant defense was enhanced (29% higher superoxide dismutase activity). These results demonstrate LE-ZW's integrated regulation of carbon-nitrogen metabolism and redox homeostasis under high CO<sub>2</sub>, supporting its potential for industrial CO<sub>2</sub> mitigation and bio-based production.
